design_philosophy.core_loop: Travel to real-world cities, gather clues from NPCs, identify stolen artifacts, defeat Koopa guards, return treasures to tourist information centers, answer quiz questions to verify authenticity
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Educational trivia and geographical knowledge required to progress; player must identify city location and artifact through dialogue clues rather than traditional platformer mechanics
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as Luigi on a globe-trotting adventure to rescue Mario; functioning as a detective solving world geography and landmark mysteries
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately subordinates traditional Mario platforming to educational content; treats real-world geography and cultural landmarks as the primary game loop rather than combat or exploration. Designed to compete with Carmen Sandiego by licensing Mario IP for edutainment purposes. Explicitly rejected traditional Mario design philosophy—Nintendo Japan insisted it not be confused with a real Mario game

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Low agency—progression gate is knowledge-based (identifying cities and artifacts from NPC clues) rather than skill-based. Difficulty tied to player's real-world geography knowledge rather than platformer execution

progression.upgrade_system: Gathering tools (clock, key, computer) used for specific puzzle progression; photo album accumulates completed artifacts
progression.unlock_mechanism: Must recover artifacts in five cities sequentially, then progress through Koopaling bosses blocking castle floors to reach final Bowser confrontation
replayability_hooks: Multiple cities with different artifacts encourage replaying to learn geography
Quiz questions on artifact authenticity vary by version
Different platformer/point-and-click implementations across NES/SNES/MS-DOS versions

world_building_techniques: NPC dialogue provides geographical and cultural context for real-world cities
Tourist information centers and pamphlets deliver landmark/artifact backstory
Computer database consolidates clues into structured world knowledge
Real-world geography serves as primary narrative device—game is essentially geography tutoring wrapped in Mario IP

boss_design.philosophy: Koopalings function as progression gates between castle floors; final Bowser encounter serves as climactic rescue objective. Boss encounters are secondary to the educational puzzle-solving core loop
notable_bosses: Bowser's Koopalings (multiple encounters blocking castle progression)
Bowser (final boss; defeated via cannon in SNES version, shell removal in MS-DOS/Mac versions)

faction_indicators: Bowser's Koopa minions positioned as global treasure thieves/antagonists
Local NPCs and tourist center attendants as information sources/allies
Luigi vs. Bowser framing maintains traditional Mario good/evil dichotomy
danger_signals: Bowser's PORTALS system represents technological threat to world cultural heritage
Koopa presence in real-world cities inverts Mario's expected fantasy setting
Artifact theft as stakes—real cultural landmarks treated as in-game valuables
environmental_storytelling: Real-world city layouts and famous landmarks embedded as game spaces
Tourist information centers and pamphlets convey each location's significance
Stolen artifacts' placement in levels indicates Koopa occupation/corruption of spaces
Bowser's Antarctic castle as grounded (non-magical) military headquarters represents departure from fantasy tone

sources_used: tcrf - Mario is Missing! (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor
